inline_review:
commit cbeb288b5e8dff5f6c72ce1dd9aa37dc3e0c08b9
Author: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>

selftests/cgroup: test_zswap: wait for asynchronous writeback

This commit introduces wait_for_writeback() to poll for zswap writeback
completion instead of checking the counters immediately, preventing spurious
test failures on platforms like ppc64le.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c b/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c
> --- a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c
[ ... ]
> +/*
> + * Writeback is asynchronous; poll until at least one writeback has
> + * been recorded for @cg, or until @timeout_ms has elapsed.
> + */
> +static long wait_for_writeback(const char *cg, int timeout_ms)
> +{
> +	int elapsed, count;
> +	for (elapsed = 0; elapsed < timeout_ms; elapsed += 100) {
> +		count = get_cg_wb_count(cg);

Does this truncate the return value of get_cg_wb_count?

Since get_cg_wb_count returns a long, storing the result in an int could
cause an overflow on 64-bit platforms if the writeback byte count ever
exceeds the maximum size of a signed int.

If the value overflows and becomes negative, it would falsely trigger the
error check below and abort the test.

> +
> +		if (count < 0)
> +			return -1;
> +		if (count > 0)
> +			return count;
> +
> +		usleep(100000);
> +	}
> +
> +	return 0;
> +}

commit cbeb288b5e8dff5f6c72ce1dd9aa37dc3e0c08b9
Author: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>
Date:   Thu Mar 26 03:26:58 2026 +0000

    selftests/cgroup: test_zswap: wait for asynchronous writeback
    
    zswap writeback is asynchronous, but test_zswap.c checks writeback
    counters immediately after reclaim/trigger paths. On some platforms
    (e.g. ppc64le), this can race with background writeback and cause
    spurious failures even when behavior is correct.
    
    Add wait_for_writeback() to poll get_cg_wb_count() with a bounded
    timeout, and use it in:
    
      test_zswap_writeback_one() when writeback is expected
      test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink() for the wb_group check
    
    This keeps the original before/after assertion style while making the
    tests robust against writeback completion latency.
    
    No test behavior change, selftest stability improvement only.
    
    Signed-off-by: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>
    Cc: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
    Cc: Michal Hocko <mhocko@kernel.org>
    Cc: Michal Koutný <mkoutny@suse.com>
    Cc: Muchun Song <muchun.song@linux.dev>
    Cc: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>
    Cc: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>
    Cc: Roman Gushchin <roman.gushchin@linux.dev>
    Cc: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
    Cc: Yosry Ahmed <yosryahmed@google.com>

diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c b/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c
index e96e316f7c474..e7d5dbc1ef7f6 100644
--- a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c
+++ b/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c
@@ -118,6 +118,27 @@ static char *setup_test_group_1M(const char *root, const char *name)
 	return NULL;
 }
 
+/*
+ * Writeback is asynchronous; poll until at least one writeback has
+ * been recorded for @cg, or until @timeout_ms has elapsed.
+ */
+static long wait_for_writeback(const char *cg, int timeout_ms)
+{
+	int elapsed, count;
+	for (elapsed = 0; elapsed < timeout_ms; elapsed += 100) {
+		count = get_cg_wb_count(cg);
+
+		if (count < 0)
+			return -1;
+		if (count > 0)
+			return count;
+
+		usleep(100000);
+	}
+
+	return 0;
+}
+
 /*
  * Sanity test to check that pages are written into zswap.
  */
@@ -343,7 +364,10 @@ static int test_zswap_writeback_one(const char *cgroup, bool wb)
 		return -1;
 
 	/* Verify that zswap writeback occurred only if writeback was enabled */
-	zswpwb_after = get_cg_wb_count(cgroup);
+	if (wb)
+		zswpwb_after = wait_for_writeback(cgroup, 5000);
+	else
+		zswpwb_after = get_cg_wb_count(cgroup);
 	if (zswpwb_after < 0)
 		return -1;
 
@@ -473,7 +497,7 @@ static int test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink(const char *root)
 	}
 
 	/* Verify that only zswapped memory from gwb_group has been written back */
-	if (get_cg_wb_count(wb_group) > 0 && get_cg_wb_count(zw_group) == 0)
+	if (wait_for_writeback(wb_group, 5000) > 0 && get_cg_wb_count(zw_group) == 0)
 		ret = KSFT_PASS;
 out:
 	cg_enter_current(root);

If we used `wait_for_writeback` when `!wb`, it would ALWAYS wait 5 seconds (because `count` would remain 0 if correct) and then return 0. Waiting 5 seconds for every negative test is terrible for test suite performance. That's why the author chose to just use `get_cg_wb_count()` when `!wb`.
However, if it *was* incorrectly triggered asynchronously, we might not catch it.
But if it's disabled, it should fail the reclaim with `-EAGAIN` right away, and not even queue writeback.
So this is likely acceptable.

    "content": "spt`cg_read` reads a string into `buf`.
```c
int cg_read(const char *cgroup, const char *control, char *buf, size_t len)
{
	char path[PATH_MAX];
	ssize_t ret;

	snprintf(path, sizeof(path), "%s/%s", cgroup, control);

	ret = read_text(path, buf, len);
	return ret == -1 ? -1 : 0;
}
```
`read_text` reads from `path` to `buf`.
It manages file descriptors correctly.
The problem:
`long cg_read_key_long(const char *cgroup, const char *control, const char *key)` returns `long`.
```c
long cg_read_key_long(const char *cgroup, const char *control, const char *key)
{
	char buf[BUF_SIZE];
	char *ptr;

	if (cg_read(cgroup, control, buf, sizeof(buf)))
		return -1;

	ptr = strstr(buf, key);
	if (!ptr)
		return -1;

	return atol(ptr + strlen(key));
}
```
If `key` is found but value is 0, it returns 0.
If `key` is not found, it returns -1.
If `cg_read_key_long` returns a very large positive number, it's fine.
However, in `wait_for_writeback`:
```c
	int elapsed, count;
	for (elapsed = 0; elapsed < timeout_ms; elapsed += 100) {
		count = get_cg_wb_count(cg);
```
Here `count` is `int`. If `get_cg_wb_count` returns `> INT_MAX`, `count` overflows. This is the truncation issue I identified.

Wait, is there an issue with `usleep(100000)`?
In `<unistd.h>`, `usleep` takes `useconds_t` which is an unsigned int. 100000 is perfectly valid.

Let's check `test_zswap_writeback_one`.
```c
	/* Verify that zswap writeback occurred only if writeback was enabled */
	if (wb)
		zswpwb_after = wait_for_writeback(cgroup, 5000);
	else
		zswpwb_after = get_cg_wb_count(cgroup);
	if (zswpwb_after < 0)
		return -1;

	if (wb != !!zswpwb_after) {
```
If `wb` is `true`, `zswpwb_after` gets the return value of `wait_for_writeback()`.
If `wait_for_writeback` succeeds and `count > 0`, it returns `count`.
`zswpwb_after` becomes `count`.
Then `!!zswpwb_after` is `!!count` which is `1`.
`wb` is `true`. `true != 1` evaluates to `false`. So it skips the `if` and returns 0.
If `wait_for_writeback` fails to reach `count > 0` and times out, it returns `0`.
`zswpwb_after = 0`. `!!zswpwb_after = 0`. `true != 0` is `true`. It returns -1.
Wait, what if `get_cg_wb_count` fails (e.g. returns -1) during `wait_for_writeback`?
`wait_for_writeback` returns `-1`.
`zswpwb_after = -1`.
`zswpwb_after < 0` is `true`, it returns `-1`.
This perfectly handles error propagation.

What if `wb` is `false`?
`zswpwb_after = get_cg_wb_count(cgroup);`
It returns whatever count it has. Since writeback shouldn't have occurred, it should be 0.
If `zswpwb_after < 0`, it returns -1.
If `zswpwb_after > 0`, `!!zswpwb_after = 1`. `false != 1` is `true`. It prints error and returns -1.
This is also correct.

Wait! What if `get_cg_wb_count` returns a huge value that gets truncated in `wait_for_writeback` but not in `get_cg_wb_count(cgroup)`?
Well, since it's a test for `cgroup/test_zswap.c`, the writeback amount is the number of bytes written to swap from `zswap`. The memory is limited. `allocation_size` is typically 1MB. `int` overflow would require >2GB writeback, which won't happen here.

Is there any issue with `usleep` being used in `wait_for_writeback`?
`usleep` suspends the process.
What if `timeout_ms` is passed as a negative value?
It's passed as 5000, so no.

Wait! In `test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink`:
```c
	/* Verify that only zswapped memory from gwb_group has been written back */
	if (wait_for_writeback(wb_group, 5000) > 0 && get_cg_wb_count(zw_group) == 0)
		ret = KSFT_PASS;
out:
```
Wait! What happens if `get_cg_wb_count(zw_group)` returns a negative value (error)?
If `get_cg_wb_count(zw_group)` returns `< 0`, `get_cg_wb_count(zw_group) == 0` evaluates to `false`.
Then `ret = KSFT_PASS` is not executed.
The test falls through to `out:` and returns `ret` (which is `KSFT_FAIL`).
This correctly fails the test on error.

What if `wait_for_writeback(wb_group, 5000) > 0`?
If it times out, it returns 0. `0 > 0` is `false`. Test fails.
If it errors, it returns -1. `-1 > 0` is `false`. Test fails.
If it succeeds, it returns `count > 0`. Test proceeds to check `zw_group`.
This is also correct.

Let's review the use of `get_cg_wb_count`:
```c
static long get_cg_wb_count(const char *cg)
{
	return cg_read_key_long(cg, "memory.stat", "zswpwb");
}
```
Is there any chance `zswpwb` might be partially matching something else?
`cg_read_key_long` uses `strstr(buf, key)`. The `key` here is `"zswpwb"`.
In `memory.stat`, is there any other key like `zswpwb_foo`?
`memory.stat` contains keys like `zswpwb`, `zswpout`, etc. I think it's just `zswpwb` for zswap writeback.
Wait, what if `cg_read_key_long` finds `zswpwb` but then it reads the number...
Ah, in `get_zswpout`, it uses `"zswpout "` (with a trailing space).
`get_cg_wb_count` uses `"zswpwb"` (no trailing space).
If there was another key like `zswpwb_bar`, it might match it. But `zswpwb` is the actual key in `memory.stat` (which usually prints `zswpwb 1234\n`). So `strstr` matches the first occurrence. It shouldn't be an issue here.

Let's think about `elapsed` vs `timeout_ms`.
`for (elapsed = 0; elapsed < timeout_ms; elapsed += 100)`
If `timeout_ms` is 5000, the condition `elapsed < 5000` is true for 0, 100, 200, ..., 4900.
That's 50 iterations.
50 iterations * 100ms = 5000ms. Correct.
Wait, is there an issue with the actual time taken? `get_cg_wb_count` takes some time. So the total wait might be larger than `timeout_ms`. That is perfectly fine for a timeout.

Could there be a bug with the variable initialization?
```c
static long wait_for_writeback(const char *cg, int timeout_ms)
{
	int elapsed, count;
...
```
`count` is initialized in the loop. It's fine.

Are there any deadlocks or locking issues?
This is a user-space selftest, no locks are involved in this test file except those hidden in the kernel syscalls.

Let's review `test_zswap_writeback_one`.
```c
	zswpwb_before = get_cg_wb_count(cgroup);
	if (zswpwb_before != 0) {
		ksft_print_msg("zswpwb_before = %ld instead of 0\n", zswpwb_before);
		return -1;
	}

	if (cg_run(cgroup, attempt_writeback, (void *) &wb))
		return -1;

	/* Verify that zswap writeback occurred only if writeback was enabled */
	if (wb)
		zswpwb_after = wait_for_writeback(cgroup, 5000);
	else
		zswpwb_after = get_cg_wb_count(cgroup);
```
Wait, `attempt_writeback` is run in a child process via `cg_run`.
It forks, enters the cgroup, runs `attempt_writeback`, and waits for the child.
When `cg_run` returns, the child has completed.

Writeback is asynchronous in the kernel. So when the child completes, writeback might still be ongoing.

int cg_run(const char *cgroup,
	   int (*fn)(const char *cgroup, void *arg),
	   void *arg)
{
	int pid, retcode;

	pid = fork();
	if (pid < 0) {
		return pid;
	} else if (pid == 0) {
		char buf[64];

		snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "%d", getpid());
		if (cg_write(cgroup, "cgroup.procs", buf))

		exit(fn(cgroup, arg));
	} else {
		waitpid(pid, &retcode, 0);
		if (WIFEXITED(retcode))
			return WEXITSTATUS(retcode);
		else
			return -1;
	}
}
```

long cg_read_key_long(const char *cgroup, const char *control, const char *key)

```
Wait! `wait_for_writeback` returns `long`.
Is `> 0` safe for `long`? Yes.
Does `wait_for_writeback` return `-1` on error? Yes.
If it returns `-1`, `-1 > 0` is false. So it fails the test. This is correct.

What about `!!zswpwb_after`?
`zswpwb_after` is `long`. `!!` converts any non-zero value to `1` (which is an `int`).
Then `wb` is `bool`.
In C, `bool` promotes to `int` when compared to `int`.
So `bool wb = true` promotes to `1`. `1 != 1` is false.
`bool wb = false` promotes to `0`. `0 != 0` is false.
This is perfectly safe.

Is there any missing `return` in `wait_for_writeback`?
It returns `-1`, `count`, or `0`. All paths are covered.

Could `usleep` block indefinitely?
No.

Could `get_cg_wb_count` block indefinitely?
It reads a sysfs/cgroup file. It shouldn't block indefinitely.

What if `wb` is `true` but writeback is instantaneous, so it finishes before `wait_for_writeback` is called?
Then `count` is positive on the very first iteration, and `wait_for_writeback` returns immediately. No delay. This is optimal.

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch for `tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c`. It looks like it's introducing a function called `wait_for_writeback()`. The goal is to asynchronously wait for writeback operations to complete, replacing the more direct `get_cg_wb_count()` calls in a couple of tests. That makes sense; asynchronous operations can be tricky, and this likely improves reliability by ensuring the writeback is actually *done* before proceeding with the tests.

Let's dive into `wait_for_writeback()` and see how it works. Okay, here's the code:

```c
/*
 * Writeback is asynchronous; poll until at least one writeback has
 * been recorded for @cg, or until @timeout_ms has elapsed.
 */
static long wait_for_writeback(const char *cg, int timeout_ms)
{
	int elapsed, count;
	for (elapsed = 0; elapsed < timeout_ms; elapsed += 100) {
		count = get_cg_wb_count(cg);

		if (count < 0)
			return -1;
		if (count > 0)
			return count;

		usleep(100000);
	}

	return 0;
}
```

Right, it's a polling loop. It checks `get_cg_wb_count()` periodically. The loop increments the elapsed time by 100 milliseconds in each iteration, which should be fine.  I need to double check the math, though, just to be sure. So, `usleep(100000)` sleeps for 100,000 microseconds, which is indeed 100 milliseconds. If the timeout is, say, 5000 milliseconds (5 seconds), the loop will run 50 times (5000 / 100). That's what I expect. 50 iterations * 100 ms = 5000 ms. Yep, that seems perfectly correct.

Okay, now let me just make sure I understand where `get_cg_wb_count()` is defined and used. I'll need to check the definition. Let's make sure it's in `test_zswap.c` as expected.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Writeback Testing and Potential Integer Overflow**

Okay, so I've been diving into this writeback test code, and here's the breakdown of my thinking. Initially, I was looking at the basic logic: if `count` is positive, just return it. Then I got to thinking, what if `count` gets messed up but we still need it. In looking at `test_zswap_writeback_one`, I made sure that the tests set `get_cg_wb_count(cgroup)` to zero at the start. So it seems that a count > 0 is correct, based on prior zeroing. I looked at `test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink`, and confirmed that newly created cgroups start with a writeback count of zero, so the initial condition is sound there.

The loop condition in `wait_for_writeback` is fine; the `elapsed` increment and `usleep` calls are correctly synchronized. The case where `get_cg_wb_count` returns zero initially is correctly handled; the loop just runs until the timeout. The possibility of `usleep` being interrupted is something to consider, but it's a self-test, so it's probably acceptable. There are no obvious leaks from code here, and the cleanup paths are correct.

I was concerned whether `get_cg_wb_count` might return something positive *before* `wait_for_writeback` is called, but the tests seem to prevent this and initialize `zswpwb_before` to 0. It seems the testing is only looking for at least one writeback to occur, not the completion of all writebacks. The test checks just `zswpwb_after > 0`.

Then I reviewed `attempt_writeback` just to make sure.

My main concern now centers on the type of `count` in `wait_for_writeback`. I realize `get_cg_wb_count` returns a `long`, but `count` is declared as an `int`. This is potentially a big issue. If `get_cg_wb_count` returns a value greater than `INT_MAX`, `count` will overflow and become negative, causing `count < 0` to incorrectly return `-1`. This would cause the test to fail if a sufficiently large amount of writeback occurs, the writeback counter overflows the int.

I started to check if the value of `zswpwb` is in pages or bytes. Since the allocations are relatively small (1MB or similar), it is likely to be ok. But `zswpwb` is a cumulative counter over the life of the cgroup? In the test, cgroups are freshly created, so the counter is reset. Still, there could be a case where a very high amount of writeback occurs.

My conclusion is that this *is* a real issue: truncating a `long` to an `int` could lead to an integer overflow. I need to be absolutely sure that the allocated sizes will never trigger this, or this could lead to incorrect test results. I will look at the exact values read by the code and how `cg_read_key_long` works to have additional information.
